coredata.dc:title: Green biosynthesis of carbon quantum dots from lotus seed plumules for folic acid detection and bioimaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yang
Wang, Xuchao
Wang, Gang
Liu, Binbin
Zhang, Xinan
Chen, Jueling
Dou, Haomiao
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a novel green hydrothermal synthesis approach using lotus seed plumules to prepare carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs) and systematically evaluates their potential applications in folic acid (FA) detection and bioimaging. L-CQDs, enriched with phytochemical constituents such as alkaloids and flavonoids, exhibit superior optical properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. For FA detection, L-CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.27 μM with a binding constant (KSV ​) of 4271.04 L·mol−1. Their practical applicability was confirmed through successful FA detection in human serum samples. Investigations into fluorescence lifetime, NMR, and zeta potential revealed that the interaction between L-CQDs and FA followed a static quenching mechanism, forming stable complexes. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments validated their low toxicity and biosafety, alongside significant imaging capabilities. These findings establish L-CQDs as efficient and multifunctional fluorescent probes for FA detection and biomedical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)
Lotus Seed Plumules
Folic Acid Detection
Bioimaging
Green Synthesis
Biocompatibility

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of L-CQDs by FA
Detection of FA in Real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2024-MS-088), the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . Schematic representation of the green hydrothermal synthesis process of L-CQDs from lotus seed plumules, highlighting the role of natural phytochemicals in enhancing fluorescence and functionality.

. Characterization of L-CQDs. (A): TEM image showing uniform spherical particles with an average size of 0.76 μm; (B): EDS spectrum confirming carbon as the dominant element (97.48 %), with minor amounts of oxygen and nitrogen; (C): XRD pattern revealing a broad peak at 2θ = 22.32°, indicative of sp2-hybridized carbon; (D): 1H NMR spectrum identifying surface functional groups (–OH, –NH2, –CH2-O, and –CH3); (E): FT-IR spectrum showing hydroxyl and sp2-hybridized carbon vibrations; (F): UV–Vis absorption spectrum; (G): PL spectra exhibiting excitation-dependent fluorescence with maximum emission at 365 nm.

. XPS analysis of L-CQDs. (A): Survey spectrum confirming the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen; (B): O1s spectrum indicating O–H and C=O bonds; (C): N1s spectrum revealing N–H and C-N-C bonds; (D): C1s spectrum deconvoluted into peaks for C=O, C-O/C-N, and sp2-hybridized carbon bonds.

. Optimization and stability tests of L-CQDs. (A, B) Optimal synthesis conditions at 200 °C for 12 h, achieving maximum fluorescence; (C) pH variation before and after synthesis; (D) Fluorescence stability under near-neutral pH conditions; (E) Resistance to X-ray-induced fluorescence degradation; (F) High salt tolerance (up to 6.0 mol/L NaCl); (G) Long-term fluorescence stability over 60 days of storage.

. FA detection and fluorescence quenching mechanism. (A, B) Specific fluorescence quenching of L-CQDs by FA among tested ions and amino acids; (C) Selectivity for FA compared to other B vitamins; (D) 1H NMR spectrum showing interactions between FA and L-CQDs; (E) Fluorescence lifetime confirming a static quenching mechanism; (F) Zeta potential changes indicating charge neutralization upon FA binding; (G) UV–Vis absorption spectrum showing reduced intensity due to complex formation.

. Detection of FA using L-CQDs. (A) Optimization of reaction time: fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs + FA (50 μmol⋅L−1) measured at 1 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, and 30 min, compared with the blank group (no FA added). (B) Fluorescence intensity across different temperatures. The intensity remained consistent from 20 °C to 70 °C, with a slight decline at 80 °C. (C) Fluorescence intensity under varying pH conditions. (D) 3D fluorescence spectra of L-CQDs with varying FA concentrations (0–100 μM), showing a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing FA concentration; (E) Calibration curve based on the Stern-Volmer equation (F/F0) as a function of FA concentration, displaying a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.9961) with a slope of −0.0063.

. Toxicity assessment of L-CQDs: (A) H&E staining of liver and kidney tissues from mice treated with varying L-CQDs doses, showing no structural abnormalities; (B, C) CCK-8 assay results indicating > 94 % cell viability for MC38 and H9C2 cells treated with L-CQDs.
. Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of MC38 cells incubated with L-CQDs solution for 24 h.
. Representative chemical structures of key phytochemicals in lotus seed plumules: alkaloids, flavonoids, and sterols.
Detection of FA in Human serum samples.
Comparison of Different Systems in FA Sensing.

head.ce:title: Green biosynthesis of carbon quantum dots from lotus seed plumules for folic acid detection and bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, 79 Chongshan East Road, Shenyang 110032 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, 79 Chongshan East Road, Shenyang 110032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiamen Cardiovascular Hospital, School of Medicine, Xiamen University, 2999 Jinshan Road, Xiamen 361015, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xiamen Cardiovascular Hospital, School of Medicine, Xiamen University, 2999 Jinshan Road, Xiamen 361015, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a novel green hydrothermal synthesis approach using lotus seed plumules to prepare carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs) and systematically evaluates their potential applications in folic acid (FA) detection and bioimaging. L-CQDs, enriched with phytochemical constituents such as alkaloids and flavonoids, exhibit superior optical properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. For FA detection, L-CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.27 μM with a binding constant (KSV ​) of 4271.04 L·mol−1. Their practical applicability was confirmed through successful FA detection in human serum samples. Investigations into fluorescence lifetime, NMR, and zeta potential revealed that the interaction between L-CQDs and FA followed a static quenching mechanism, forming stable complexes. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments validated their low toxicity and biosafety, alongside significant imaging capabilities. These findings establish L-CQDs as efficient and multifunctional fluorescent probes for FA detection and biomedical research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (vitamin B9) is a water-soluble vitamin that plays a vital role in carbon biosynthesis and epigenetic processes. Clinically, folic acid deficiency has been associated with various health issues, including neural tube defects [1], megaloblastic anemia [2], cardiovascular diseases [3], depression [4,5], cognitive disorders [6], and increased cancer risk [7]. Traditional methods for detecting folic acid, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], microbiological assays, radioimmunoassay (RIA) [9], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), are often complex, requiring stringent environmental conditions, advanced equipment, and high-quality reagents. Thus, there is an urgent need for a high-efficiency, green, and cost-effective alternative.

Quantum dots (QDs) are extensively studied in biosensors due to their unique optical properties, such as high brightness, broad absorption spectra, and narrow emission bands [10]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly attractive due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties [11,12]. Green synthesis has become a key focus in nanotechnology, aiming to produce environmentally friendly and sustainable carbon nanomaterials [13–15]. Natural and renewable resources, such as centella [16], milk [17], and banana stems [18], have been successfully utilized for CQD synthesis, representing eco-friendly and sustainable approaches [19], which further enhance their clinical applicability.

Lotus seed plumules, a natural plant-based biomass material, are rich in alkaloids and flavonoids [20–22], making them a widely available and cost-effective precursor. In this study, lotus seed plumules were used as the raw material to synthesize L-CQDs through a green hydrothermal method [23] (Fig. 1 ). The resulting L-CQDs exhibited excellent optical properties and stability, maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity across different pH conditions. Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of L-CQDs enable specific binding to folic acid molecules, demonstrating high sensitivity and selectivity.

The aim of this study is to characterize the optical properties of L-CQDs and their interaction with folic acid, exploring their potential application as fluorescent probes for clinical folic acid detection. Additionally, the study evaluates their functionality in bioimaging, offering new insights into the development of green and sustainable biomedical materials.

Lotus seed plumules were purchased from a local supermarket. Human serum samples used in this study were approved by the Ethics Committee of the First Hospital of Jilin University. Mouse colon adenocarcinoma cells (MC38) and rat cardiomyocyte H9C2 cells were obtained from iCell Bioscience Inc. (Shanghai, China). RPMI 1640 medium was purchased from Basalmedia (Shanghai, China), and distilled water was supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Drinking water was sourced from Wahaha Group Co., Ltd. Analytical-grade reagents, including sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl), were provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory and RionLon Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Folic acid (purity: 97 %) and other analytical-grade amino acids and vitamins were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hematoxylin and eosin staining kits were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China).

High-precision instruments were employed for the characterization and detection of L-CQDs. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1810 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd.). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan) to analyze the size and morphology of L-CQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Elemental composition was analyzed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) system. Confocal fluorescence imaging experiments were carried out using a STELLARIS 5 laser confocal microscope (Leica, Germany), and tissue quantitative analysis was conducted using the TissueFAXS system (TissueGnostics, Austria). All instruments were calibrated and operated according to standard protocols.

L-CQDs were synthesized from lotus seed cores using a one-step hydrothermal method. The lotus seed cores were ultrasonically cleaned with purified water, dried, and ground into a fine powder. Subsequently, 5 g of the fine powder was mixed with 10 mL of distilled water to form a slurry. This slurry was transferred into a 20 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove residual reactants and particulates, ensuring product purification. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to yield a yellow–brown L-CQD stock solution, which was subsequently used for characterization and application studies. Because the L-CQDs were synthesized from natural biomaterials without any passivating agents, they required storage in a light-protected environment. To ensure stability, the centrifuge tube containing the stock solution was wrapped in aluminum foil.

where RPM is the centrifuge speed (revolutions per minute), r is the radius of the centrifuge rotor (in centimeters), and g is the gravitational acceleration (9.8 m/s2) [24].

where I0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of folic acid, I is the fluorescence intensity in its presence, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, which indicates the efficiency of fluorescence quenching, and [Q] is the concentration of folic acid [27].

where FA Dosage is the FA concentration actually added in the experiment, and FA Test Amount is the FA concentration detected through the experiment.

Where SD (Standard Deviation) represents the variability or dispersion of the dataset and is calculated as:

Here,

is the mean of the dataset, and

6 Limit of detection (LOD)

3 σ

where

is the standard deviation of the fluorescence response, and

is the slope of the calibration curve.

7 Cell Viability
C e l l V i a b i l i t y
%

A b
× 100 %

where
A s
is the absorbance of the sample,
A b
is the absorbance of the blank group, and
A c
is the absorbance of the control group.

Selectivity is a fundamental parameter for the practical detection of FA using L-CQDs. To investigate the selectivity of L-CQDs, a concentration of 5 mM was used to test various common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, H+, NH4 +, Cl−, HCO3 −) and amino acids (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Thr, Met, Gln, Lys). L-CQDs were added to these solutions, and the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times to ensure reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of L-CQDs by FA
ce:section.ce:para: A series of FA standard solutions with concentrations ranging from 0.0 to 100.0 μM/mL was prepared, with increments of 5.0 μM/mL. For each 1 mL FA solution, 100 μL of L-CQD stock solution was added and vortexed to ensure uniform mixing. The fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Each experiment was performed in triplicate to ensure reliability.

Three different human serum samples were used, each diluted 100-fold. FA solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL were added to the diluted serum samples, followed by the addition of L-CQDs. The fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The recovery rates of FA in human serum were calculated based on the linear fluorescence response of L-CQDs. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure reliability.

Mouse colon adenocarcinoma cells (MC38) and rat cardiomyocyte H9C2 cells were obtained from Sigma. The cells were cultured in DMEM (GIBCO) medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, GIBCO), 1 % penicillin, and 1 % streptomycin. They were maintained in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2​. For optimal growth, cells were cultured in standard T25 flasks until they reached 80–90 % confluence, with the medium changed every two days. Prior to reaching confluence, cells were digested with trypsin and seeded at a concentration of 1 × 105 cells per dish or well.

The biocompatibility and toxicity of L-CQDs were assessed using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Eight-week-old male C57BL6/J mice were housed in pathogen-free facilities under controlled conditions (22–23 °C, 12-hour light/dark cycle). All experiments followed the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals provided by the National Institutes of Health and were approved by the Animal Care and Protection Committee of Xiamen University (protocol number XMULAC20200150).

Mice were orally administered L-CQDs at concentrations of 0, 50, 100, and 200 µg/kg/day for seven consecutive days. Following ethical guidelines, euthanasia was performed using CO2​ inhalation. Liver and kidney tissues were collected and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Paraffin-embedded sections were prepared and stained with H&E to examine morphological changes and tissue damage in the liver and kidneys exposed to different concentrations of L-CQDs.

In vitro cytotoxicity of L-CQDs was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay to determine cell viability following L-CQDs treatment. Mouse colon adenocarcinoma MC38 cells and rat cardiomyocyte H9C2 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then exposed to L-CQD solutions at concentrations of 0, 15, 30, 60, and 120 μg/mL and incubated for an additional 12 h at 37 °C. After incubation, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for another 2 h. Absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader, and the data were recorded.

MC38 cells were seeded onto four-chamber culture slides at a density of 5 × 105 cells per chamber and incubated for 24 h. Once the cells reached approximately 70 % confluence, they were treated with L-CQD solutions at concentrations of 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL and incubated for an additional 24 h. Following treatment, the culture medium was aspirated, and the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). The cells were then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution at room temperature for 30 min and washed three more times with PBS. The nuclei were stained using TO-PRO-3, and the slides were mounted using Fluoroshield™ mounting medium. The slides were stored overnight in a dark and dry environment to optimize imaging conditions. The next day, cell imaging was performed using a STELLARIS 5 laser confocal microscope. Images were captured with a 20 × objective lens, and the scale bar represents 100 μm.

The morphology and elemental composition of L-CQDs were confirmed using TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 A. The images revealed uniform spherical particles with an average size of approximately 0.76 μm. The lattice spacing was measured to be 3.75 Å. The crystalline size of L-CQDs was about 0.31 nm, indicating their nanoscale dimensions.

EDS characterization Fig. 2B demonstrated that the CQDs primarily consisted of carbon (C content: 97.48 %, Atom%: 98.36 %), indicating a typical carbon-based structure. Minor amounts of oxygen (0.69 %) and nitrogen (0.79 %) were also detected, suggesting the presence of oxygen-containing groups (e.g., hydroxyl or carboxyl) and nitrogen-doped functional groups (e.g., amino groups) on the CQD surface. These functional groups enhance the surface chemical activity and water solubility of CQDs [29]. The presence of Si and K likely originates from natural silicon and potassium in the lotus seed plumules. These elements were retained as trace impurities or doping components during CQD preparation and did not significantly affect their performance.

The XRD pattern of L-CQDs (Fig. 2C) exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22.32°, indicating the graphite-like nature of sp2 hybridization [30]. The 1H NMR spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 2D) provided insights into the surface functional groups [31]: Signal A indicated the presence of –OH or –NH2 groups. Signal B corresponded to coupling modes of –CH2-NH2​ or –CH2​–OH. Signal C was attributed to –CH2-O near alkyl chains. Signal D represented –CH3​ groups in alkyl chains. Signal E was characteristic of alkyl protons in nonpolar environments, suggesting that L-CQDs exhibit some hydrophobicity.

FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2E) further identified the surface functional groups of L-CQDs. A broad peak at approximately 3317 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of − OH groups, while the peak at 1637 cm−1 was attributed to C=C stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of sp2 hybridization [32]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2F) showed absorption peaks at 225 nm and 252 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions of C=O and C=C bonds, respectively. Under daylight and UV light (365 nm), L-CQDs emitted yellow and blue fluorescence, respectively [33]. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield of L-CQDs was calculated to be 0.86 %. Fig. 2G illustrates the PL spectrum of L-CQDs over an excitation wavelength range of 300–440 nm, with the maximum PL intensity observed at 365 nm. The emission peak showed significant variation with the excitation wavelength, indicating excitation-dependent emission behavior.

The elemental composition of L-CQDs was analyzed via XPS (Fig. 3 A). Peaks corresponding to oxygen (530 eV), nitrogen (399 eV), and carbon (284 eV) were observed [34]. Additional peaks at 152 eV and 208 eV originated from tin (Sn) and indium (In) in the ITO substrate used during sample preparation. A minor Si peak at 105 eV was attributed to natural silicon in the lotus seed plumules. Detailed XPS analysis provided further insights: The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) displayed peaks at 532.1 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to O–H and C=O bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) showed peaks at 401.2 eV and 399.3 eV, assigned to N–H and C-N-C bonds. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) revealed three peaks at 288 eV, 285 eV, and 284 eV, corresponding to C=O/C=N, C-O/C-N, and C=C/C–C bonds, respectively. These results confirm that L-CQDs primarily consist of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Carbon forms the nanostructure's backbone, nitrogen enhances activity and fluorescence intensity, and oxygen contributes to excellent water solubility and biocompatibility.

To determine the optimal experimental conditions for L-CQD synthesis and stability, parameters such as reaction time, temperature, pH, light exposure duration, and salt concentration were systematically investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, L-CQDs synthesized at 200 °C for 12 h exhibited the best fluorescence performance. A significant pH decrease was observed before and after synthesis, with the pH values dropping from 6.74 to 5.32, as shown in Fig. 4C. Fig. 4D demonstrates that L-CQDs maintain stable fluorescence intensity under near-neutral pH conditions, while fluorescence is significantly affected in highly acidic or alkaline environments.

As indicated in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs remained unaffected by prolonged X-ray exposure, demonstrating excellent photostability. Fig. 4F shows that even at high salt concentrations of 6.0 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs remained stable, highlighting their strong resistance to salinity. Finally, as shown in Fig. 4G, the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs did not exhibit any significant decline after 60 days of storage, underscoring their high stability and suitability for long-term applications.

To evaluate their potential as fluorescent probes, the anti-interference ability of L-CQDs was assessed using various ions and amino acids. As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, only folic acid (FA) induced significant fluorescence quenching in L-CQDs, demonstrating their strong anti-interference capability. Since FA belongs to the vitamin B family, ions related to the vitamin B complex were also tested for selective detection using L-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5C, FA exhibited the most pronounced fluorescence quenching effect, highlighting the excellent selectivity of L-CQDs for FA detection.

To investigate the quenching mechanism of FA on L-CQDs, NMR characterization was performed. From the 1H NMR results of L-CQDs + FA (Fig. 5D), signal A indicated that polar groups on the L-CQDs surface (–OH or –NH2) might interact with polar groups of FA via hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions. Signal B corresponded to –CH-NH or a similar environment in FA, suggesting the involvement of the amide group in the binding. Signal C was attributed to protons from the aliphatic chain of FA, while signal D represented residual groups in FA. By comparing Fig. 2D, it can be inferred that L-CQDs and FA form stable non-emissive complexes through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π stacking, leading to fluorescence quenching. This fluorescence quenching is primarily attributed to a static quenching mechanism [35], where L-CQDs and FA interact in the ground state to form complexes, preventing effective excitation and fluorescence emission. Additionally, π-π stacking alters the electronic structure of L-CQDs, reducing fluorescence emission efficiency [36]. A minor contribution from dynamic quenching, where FA absorbs the excitation energy of L-CQDs and dissipates it non-radiatively, might also occur but is less significant. Thus, the binding of FA to L-CQDs significantly reduces fluorescence signals, predominantly due to static quenching.

Fluorescence lifetime, defined as the average time molecules remain in the excited state before returning to the ground state by emitting photons [37], was also used to study the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5E, the fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs was 5.99 ns. After the addition of FA, the lifetime decreased slightly to 5.19 ns. The minimal change in fluorescence lifetime confirms that FA does not quench the fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs, indicating a static quenching mechanism where quenching occurs due to ground-state complex formation between L-CQDs and FA [38].

To further investigate the quenching mechanism, zeta potential measurements were conducted to analyze the surface charge of L-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5F, the surface charge of L-CQDs was nearly neutral. Upon mixing with FA, the zeta potential changed significantly from −0.628 mV and −36.9 mV to −26.4 mV. This significant change is attributed to strong interactions and charge transfer between FA molecules and the surface of L-CQDs. The negative functional groups of FA partially neutralized the negative charges on the surface of L-CQDs, reducing the overall negative charge of the mixture [39]. These results indicate notable surface modifications and interactions between FA and L-CQDs, supporting the existence of static binding.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 5G reveals characteristic absorption peaks of L-CQDs at 225 nm and 252 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions of C=O and C=C bonds, respectively [40]. These peaks indicate that L-CQDs possess a rich conjugated structure and active surface functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups), providing a solid foundation for their optical properties. FA exhibited a broad absorption band around 280 nm, characteristic of its aromatic structure, though its absorbance was relatively low. Upon forming a complex with FA (L-CQDs + FA), the overall absorption intensity decreased. The absorption peaks of L-CQDs at 225 nm and 252 nm weakened significantly, and the characteristic absorption band of FA at 280 nm also diminished. This suggests strong interactions between L-CQDs and FA, likely through static binding to form a stable complex that perturbs the electronic distribution of L-CQDs. Specifically, the aromatic structure of FA may interact with the conjugated system of L-CQDs via π-π stacking. Additionally, the carboxyl (–COOH) and amide (–CONH2) groups in FA may interact with functional groups on the L-CQDs surface via electron transfer, reducing fluorescence emission efficiency [41].

Based on these experimental results and analyses, it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching of L-CQDs by FA primarily occurs through a static quenching mechanism. FA interacts with the surface of L-CQDs via π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic interactions to form stable complexes. Surface charge transfer and electrostatic interactions further stabilize the binding, effectively explaining the strong binding affinity and fluorescence quenching phenomena of L-CQDs and FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FA in Real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions for FA (50 μmol⋅L−1) using L-CQDs were optimized by evaluating the reaction time (Fig. 6 A), temperature (Fig. 6B), and pH environment (Fig. 6C). The results demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity remains stable across different reaction times (1–30 min) and temperatures (20–70 °C), with a slight decrease observed at 80 °C. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity is consistent in a broad pH range (3–11), highlighting the robustness and versatility of L-CQDs for FA detection under varying experimental conditions. These findings confirm the reliability of L-CQDs in practical applications.

To evaluate the practical detection performance of L-CQDs, three different human serum samples were tested, each diluted 100-fold. As shown in Table 1
(Supplementary Table 2), the spiked recovery rates of FA ranged from 97.4 % to 103.4 %, demonstrating high recovery efficiency. These results confirm that L-CQDs are well-suited for detecting FA in human serum, highlighting their potential for clinical applications. Furthermore, comparisons with other methods in the literature (Table 2) indicate that the L-CQD-based FA detection method exhibits high sensitivity and specificity, emphasizing its advantages for clinical diagnostics.

The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of L-CQDs were evaluated through in vitro and in vivo experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 A, H&E staining results revealed no significant differences in liver and kidney tissues of mice treated with L-CQDs via oral gavage for seven days compared to the control group. Even at high doses of 200 µg/kg/day, the liver and kidney tissues displayed intact structures, no fibrosis, normal cell morphology, and no evident inflammatory cell infiltration. These findings indicate that L-CQDs do not cause pathological damage to the hepatic and renal metabolic functions of mice.

Additionally, the effects of L-CQDs at various concentrations (15–120 μg/mL) on cell viability were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7B and C, the viability of MC38 cells was 94.25 %, while that of H9C2 cells was 95.1 %. These results confirm that L-CQDs exhibit low toxicity in both in vitro and in vivo environments, further validating their excellent biocompatibility.

Laser confocal microscopy was employed to investigate the bioimaging potential of L-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8 , MC38 cells incubated with L-CQD solutions for 24 h exhibited blue fluorescence. In contrast, the control group, which was not incubated with L-CQDs, showed no detectable fluorescence in the L-CQD channel, confirming that the observed fluorescence in the experimental group originated from L-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity increased as the L-CQD concentration was elevated from 50 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL, demonstrating a dose-dependent uptake of L-CQDs by the cells. The overlap of L-CQD fluorescence with TO-PRO-3-stained cell nuclei indicates that L-CQDs localized to the perinuclear region, suggesting that L-CQDs were effectively internalized by the cells and likely resided in the cytoplasm.

In summary, the images demonstrate the effective internalization of L-CQDs by cells and their dose-dependent uptake. This highlights the potential of L-CQDs for cellular imaging applications. Furthermore, the presence of L-CQD fluorescence near the cell nucleus suggests their utility for labeling and tracking cellular components, providing valuable insights for bioimaging research.

In this study, carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs) were synthesized using lotus seed plumules as raw material through a green and sustainable one-step hydrothermal method. The potential of L-CQDs for folic acid (FA) detection and bioimaging was systematically evaluated. Lotus seed plumules, as a natural plant-based material, are rich in phytochemicals such as alkaloids, flavonoids, and sterols (Fig. 9 ) [47]. These compounds played critical roles during the synthesis of L-CQDs: 1. Alkaloids. Rich in carbon and nitrogen (e.g., pyridine and indole units), they form the primary backbone of L-CQDs through carbonization and nitrogen doping at high temperatures [48]. Nitrogen doping enhances the fluorescence performance and surface activity of L-CQDs, improving quantum yield and water solubility. Functional groups such as amines and pyridine nitrogen from alkaloids contribute to surface functionalization, improving chemical stability and dispersibility. 2. Flavonoids. Containing polyphenol structures, flavonoids serve as carbon sources during pyrolysis and facilitate L-CQD formation through redox reactions [49]. The hydroxyl groups in these polyphenols can form surface hydroxyl (–OH) groups at high temperatures, while their conjugated π-electron systems influence L-CQD fluorescence properties by partially retaining optical features after decomposition. 3. Sterols. These decompose into cyclic structures, providing carbon sources and enhancing the stability of L-CQDs [50]. Their hydrophobic long chains may form hydrophobic surface regions in L-CQDs after pyrolysis, affecting dispersibility in different solvents. In summary, the phytochemicals in lotus seed plumules not only provide precursors for L-CQD synthesis but also enhance their fluorescence performance, biocompatibility, and multifunctionality by modulating physicochemical properties.

The experimental results demonstrated that L-CQDs possess unique optical properties, excellent environmental stability, and low toxicity, showcasing their significant potential for FA detection and bioimaging. Under UV excitation, L-CQDs emit blue fluorescence and exhibit remarkable stability under various environmental conditions, including neutral pH, high salt concentrations, and long-term storage. These properties ensure their reliability in complex samples and practical applications. Additionally, L-CQDs demonstrated high selectivity toward FA, overcoming the challenges associated with traditional FA detection methods, such as HPLC and ELISA, which often involve complex procedures, expensive equipment, and interference issues.

The L-CQD-based FA detection method exhibited high sensitivity and specificity, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.27 μM. It showed a broad detection range with a significant linear relationship with FA concentration. The Stern-Volmer constant (KSV ) of 4271.04 L·mol−1 indicated strong binding efficiency between L-CQDs and FA. Fluorescence lifetime measurements, NMR analysis, and zeta potential studies revealed a static quenching mechanism, where FA forms stable complexes with L-CQDs via π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions. This binding not only reduces fluorescence signals but also highlights the surface activity and functionalization potential of L-CQDs, offering insights for optimizing fluorescence sensors and designing multifunctional nanoprobes.

In practical applications, L-CQDs successfully detected FA in diluted human serum samples with spiked recovery rates ranging from 97.4 % to 103.4 %, demonstrating high sensitivity and specificity in complex biological samples. This indicates that L-CQDs are not only promising in laboratory research but also suitable for clinical FA detection, providing a rapid, economical, and portable diagnostic tool.

Furthermore, the safety of L-CQDs was comprehensively evaluated through in vitro and in vivo experiments. The results showed no significant effects on liver and kidney tissue structure or cell viability, even under high-dose oral administration in mice or high-concentration incubation at the cellular level. These findings confirm the excellent biocompatibility of L-CQDs as a biomedical tool. In cell imaging experiments, L-CQDs exhibited dose-dependent fluorescence intensity, were effectively internalized by cells, and primarily localized in the cytoplasmic region. Their strong fluorescence signals and efficient internalization suggest potential applications in cell imaging, drug delivery, and disease labeling.

In conclusion, the L-CQDs synthesized via a green hydrothermal method combine high sensitivity, environmental stability, and biological safety. They offer an efficient and economical alternative for FA detection and show promising applications in bioimaging and clinical diagnostics.
Ethics statement.

The experiments were approved by Animal Care and Protection Committee of Xiamen University (Protocol No. XMULAC20200150) and were conducted in accordance with the NIH guidelines for the Care and Use Laboratory Animal.

This work was supported by Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2024-MS-088), the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125825.
